text:
Hydrogen fuel cells represent a cornerstone for the next generation of zero-emission automotive transportation. Central to their advancement is the development of electrocatalysts that are efficient, durable, and cost-effective enough to overcome the sluggish kinetics of the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR). In this study, we report the synthesis of metal-organic framework (MOF)-derived non-precious dual-metal single-atom catalysts (Zn/Co-N-C), featuring isolated Co-N₄ and Zn-N₄ local structures. 

These catalysts demonstrate exceptional ORR activity with a half-wave potential ($E_{1/2}$) of 0.938 V vs. RHE and remarkable durability, showing a minimal shift ($\Delta E_{1/2} = -8.5$ mV) after 50,000 electrochemical cycles. The practical utility of this material was confirmed under realistic fuel cell operating conditions, where it achieved a record-breaking peak power density of $\sim$1 W cm⁻²—the highest reported to date for single-atom catalysts in alkaline fuel cells.

To gain structural and mechanistic insights, we employed *operando* X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS). Our findings identify the Co atom within the Co-N₄ architecture as the primary catalytic center, which binds an axial oxygenated species to form an $O_{ads}$-Co-N₄ moiety during the reaction. These experimental observations were supported by theoretical studies using potential-dependent microkinetic modeling and core-level shift calculations. The computational results align closely with our experimental data, elucidating the specific bonding behavior of oxygen species on Co-N₄ centers. This work establishes a comprehensive understanding of the active sites in Zn/Co-N-C catalysts and provides a strategic framework for the future design of high-performance, single-site electrocatalysts for energy conversion technologies.
